findings.description: Both SDK harnesses use `except BaseException` followed by a `yield RunFinished(error=type(exc).__name__)` from inside the except block (deep_agents.py:198-207, ms_agent_fw.py:330-337 and 387-394). This is a Python async-generator anti-pattern: `BaseException` includes `GeneratorExit`, `asyncio.CancelledError`, `KeyboardInterrupt`, and `SystemExit`. Yielding while handling `GeneratorExit` raises `RuntimeError('asynchronous generator ignored GeneratorExit')`; cancellation paths get mislabeled as 'errors' and propagate a synthesized terminal event the spec did not ask for. The route's IMPL_ITERATE fix #3 (`aclosing(harness.astream_invoke(...))`) drives exactly this finalization path on every client disconnect — the spec scenario 'Client disconnect during streaming cancels the harness' is the documented trigger. Because the disconnect test (tests/web/test_app.py:328) uses `_make_text_harness()` (a fake that just yields a fixed event list, with no `try/except BaseException` mirror of the real harness), the bug is uncovered by CI. The same gap exists in tests/harnesses/test_deep_agents_astream.py and test_ms_agent_fw_astream.py — they exercise Phase 1/2 via `_make_error_stream` (a deliberate Exception raise), never via `aclose()`/`GeneratorExit`.
findings.resolution: Narrow the catch to `except Exception as exc` in deep_agents.py:198 and ms_agent_fw.py:330 + :387 so GeneratorExit/CancelledError/KeyboardInterrupt/SystemExit propagate without being mis-mapped to a Phase-1 RunFinished(error=...). Optionally add an explicit `except GeneratorExit: raise` ahead of the Exception catch for clarity. Then add one regression test per harness that wraps the real `astream_invoke()` in `contextlib.aclosing()`, consumes one event, and calls `aclose()`; the test must assert (a) no `RuntimeError` propagates and (b) no `RunFinished(error='GeneratorExit')` event is yielded. A second test using `asyncio.CancelledError` (raised from inside the fake agent's `astream_events`) should assert that CancelledError propagates through the harness without first yielding a synthesized RunFinished — matching the new narrowed-catch contract.

findings.description: tests/cli/test_serve.py::test_serve_uses_default_role_when_r_omitted asserts `make_app_calls[0]['role'] in ('chief_of_staff', 'assistant')` — accepting either of two unrelated values. This is a tautological assertion: it does not verify the `serve` command actually pulled `default_role` off the persona config, only that the value is one of two strings. If the fallback logic in cli.py:327-332 ever regressed to e.g. hard-coding 'assistant' or reading a wrong attribute, the test would still pass. The test should look up the fixture persona's actual `default_role` value (or, better, monkeypatch the loaded PersonaConfig to set a sentinel default_role like 'sentinel-default-role-xyz' and assert exact equality) so the fallback path is genuinely exercised.
findings.resolution: Rewrite the test to inject a known sentinel default_role through the existing `_load_persona_or_fail` patch pattern used by `test_serve_no_default_role_persona_without_r` (tests/cli/test_serve.py:235-247). Set `pc.default_role = 'sentinel-default-role'` on the mocked PersonaConfig and assert `make_app_calls[0]['role'] == 'sentinel-default-role'`. This proves the default-role fallback is wired correctly and is robust to fixture churn.

findings.description: `assistant serve` constructs the harness twice. cli.py:341 calls `_create_harness(pc, rc, harness)` to validate the harness type (host-vs-SDK rejection on lines 345-351), then make_app() at cli.py:361 internally calls `create_harness(pc, rc, harness_name)` again at app.py:123. The first instance is discarded immediately. For deep_agents this is harmless; for harnesses with non-trivial __init__ side effects (e.g. seeding ChatClient credentials, opening a checkpointer) it doubles the cost and could surface ordering surprises if a future harness logs at construction time. The same host-vs-SDK rejection is enforced inside make_app (app.py:124-128), so the CLI-side rejection is redundant.
findings.resolution: Either (a) remove the CLI-side `_create_harness` validation entirely and rely on make_app's own host-rejection (`isinstance(harness, HostHarnessAdapter)`) — `make_app()` already raises `ValueError` with the same message; or (b) extend make_app to accept an optional pre-built harness (`make_app(..., _harness: HarnessAdapter | None = None)`) and pass the CLI's already-constructed instance through, mirroring the `_agent_factory` test-injection pattern. Option (a) is the smaller change and keeps the construction flow in one place.

findings.description: The non-loopback warning in cli.py:353 uses `if host != '127.0.0.1' and not host.startswith('127.')`. This misses two common loopback spellings: `::1` (IPv6 loopback — fires a 'binding to non-loopback host' warning even though `::1` IS loopback) and `localhost` (resolves to loopback on every default-configured host). The false positive on `::1` is the more interesting case: an operator binding `--host ::1` for IPv6-only is told they are exposing the server, which is wrong and may push them to weaker bindings. Conversely `localhost` triggering the warning is technically correct on locked-down hosts where `/etc/hosts` maps localhost to a non-loopback address, but the same warning will fire on every standard machine where it shouldn't.
findings.resolution: Replace the substring check with an allowlist plus `ipaddress.ip_address(host).is_loopback` lookup (wrapped in try/except for non-IP hosts like 'localhost'): treat `host` as loopback if it is exactly 'localhost' OR if `ipaddress.ip_address(host).is_loopback` returns True. Only warn otherwise. Keep the warning text the same.
